title: Caching Cost & Latency Tradeoffs
concept: Quantifying the savings from cached input tokens versus the overhead of cache management.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: what usually breaks a cache hit, according to the last lesson?

options.text: Any change earlier in the prompt's prefix, even a tiny one
Using the cache too many times in a row
Making the output shorter than expected

exercises.explain: Cache invalidation happens when anything earlier in the prefix changes — even a single token difference near the start can break the match and force a fresh (non-cached) run for everything after it.

cards.title: Is Caching Actually Worth It?
cards.body: Caching sounds like a free win — cheaper tokens, faster responses. But every cache has hidden costs: setup time, storage, and the risk of invalidation wiping it out. Today we learn how to actually weigh the savings against the overhead.

cards.title: What's the Tradeoff?
cards.body: Caching saves money because cached input tokens are billed at a steep discount compared to fresh ones. But caching isn't free — there's overhead: extra latency on the first (cold) call, memory/storage costs, and engineering complexity to keep the cache valid. The tradeoff is: does the savings from reuse outweigh that overhead?

cards.title: Meal Prep vs. Cooking Fresh
cards.body: Prepping a big batch of rice on Sunday saves time all week — but only if you actually eat rice every day. If you only eat it once, the extra prep work wasn't worth it. Caching prompt tokens is the same: it saves money and time ONLY if you reuse that prefix often enough to make the setup worthwhile.

cards.title: Do the Math
cards.body: Say your fresh input tokens cost $3 per million, but cached tokens cost $0.30 per million (a 90% discount). If you send the same 2,000-token system prompt 500 times a day, that's 1,000,000 tokens — normally $3, but cached it's about $0.30 plus a tiny one-time write cost. Multiply that daily savings by a whole month, and it adds up fast, especially at scale.

cards.title: Myth: Caching Always Makes Things Cheaper AND Faster
cards.body: Not always! The very first call that creates the cache (a 'cache write') can be slightly slower or cost slightly more than a normal call. If your prompt rarely repeats, you pay that overhead repeatedly without ever collecting the reuse savings — a net loss.

cards.title: Savings vs. Overhead
cards.body: Picture two bars: one for token savings from reuse, one for overhead cost of maintaining the cache. Caching is worth it only when the savings bar is taller.

left.text: Same 3,000-token prefix used 200x/day → tiny overhead, huge discount stacking up — clear win

right.text: Prefix changes almost every call → you pay write costs repeatedly, rarely hit the discount — likely a loss

exercises.prompt: A team caches a 4,000-token prompt prefix, but their app's prefix changes on almost every single request due to dynamic content near the start. What's most likely true?

options.text: They'll get massive savings because the prefix is long
They'll rarely benefit, since a changing prefix invalidates the cache almost every time, so they mostly pay cache-write overhead without reuse savings
Caching always beats no caching regardless of reuse rate
The cache will automatically fix the changing content to keep it valid

exercises.explain: Caching's value comes from REUSE. If the prefix keeps changing (as we learned in the invalidation lesson), the cache mostly misses — meaning they pay the write cost over and over but rarely collect the cheaper cached-read discount. Long prefixes only pay off when they're stable and reused often.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Even with caching enabled, the very first request that writes to the cache typically doesn't get the full cached-token discount.
exercises.explain: True. The first call has to actually create the cache entry (a 'cache write'), which isn't the same as a cache hit. Only requests AFTER that, which reuse the same prefix, get the cheaper cached-read pricing.

exercises.sentence: Caching only pays off when the same prefix is reused often enough to outweigh the overhead of the cache write.
exercises.explain: The word 'overhead' refers to the extra cost of creating and maintaining the cache. If reuse doesn't happen often enough, that overhead outweighs the savings — making caching a net loss.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three cost explanations was most likely AI-generated filler — technically-worded but actually vague and unhelpful for decision-making?

options.text: 'Caching leverages advanced optimization paradigms to holistically enhance token efficiency across the request lifecycle.'
'If your fixed prefix repeats 100+ times a day, the discounted cached tokens will likely save more than the one-time write cost.'
'Compare fresh-token price times prefix length against cached-token price times prefix length, times how many times you reuse it, to see your break-even point.'

exercises.explain: Option A is buzzword-heavy ('leverages', 'paradigms', 'holistically') but says nothing concrete about numbers, reuse, or tradeoffs — a classic sign of AI-generated filler that sounds smart but doesn't help you actually decide anything. The other two give real, usable reasoning.

exercises.prompt: Your app sends a 6,000-token system prompt + tool schema on every request, followed by a short unique user question. You get 1,000 requests per day, and cached tokens cost far less than fresh ones. What's the smartest way to think about whether caching helps here?

options.text: Caching never helps unless the whole prompt including the user question is identical every time
Since the 6,000-token prefix repeats across all 1,000 requests, caching it turns almost all of that cost into discounted cached tokens, while only the short unique question stays full price
Caching only matters for output tokens, not input tokens, so this setup won't benefit
You should avoid caching here because the user question changes every request, invalidating the whole prompt

exercises.explain: The key insight of this whole unit: caching works on the shared, unchanging PREFIX, not the entire request. Here, the 6,000-token system prompt/tool schema is identical across all 1,000 calls, so it's a perfect caching candidate — it gets stored once and reused (at a steep discount) again and again. Only the small unique tail (the user's question) needs full-price processing each time. The math clearly favors caching: huge repeated cost turned cheap, tiny variable cost stays as-is. That's the tradeoff to always check — repetition volume vs. management overhead — before deciding to cache. You've completed this unit on Prompt & Context Caching!
